content:
Key Findings

  • West Lancashire Borough Council paid 4,614 unique suppliers between 2016/17 and 2025/26
  • 351 suppliers received under £1,000 each, representing 7.6% of all suppliers
  • 2,607 suppliers (56.5% of the total) received between £10,000 and £100,000 each
  • Only 51 suppliers (1.1% of the total) received over £1 million each
  • The council spent £332.9 million across 43,063 transactions in this period

The Long Tail: West Lancashire's Hundreds of Small Suppliers

West Lancashire Borough Council has paid 4,614 different suppliers over the past decade, with the vast majority receiving relatively small amounts. This "long tail" of suppliers raises questions about procurement efficiency and potential savings through consolidation. The council's spending data reveals a complex web of transactions, with significant sums going to a small number of large suppliers, while thousands of others receive much smaller amounts.

The distribution of payments is highly skewed. While 51 suppliers (just 1.1% of the total) received over £1 million each, the vast majority—3,349 suppliers (72.6%)—received between £1,000 and £100,000. This fragmentation of contracts could indicate a preference for smaller, more frequent purchases or a lack of consolidation opportunities. The council's total spend of £332.9 million across 43,063 transactions suggests a high volume of relatively small transactions.

Supplier Size Distribution

The data shows a clear long tail distribution, with a small number of large suppliers and a large number of small ones. For example, 351 suppliers received under £1,000 each, while 2,607 suppliers received between £10,000 and £100,000. This distribution raises questions about the efficiency of the council's procurement processes. Consolidating purchases with fewer, larger suppliers could potentially reduce administrative overhead and negotiate better rates.

However, it's important to consider the context. Smaller suppliers may provide specialised services or goods that are not available from larger providers. The council may also prioritise supporting local businesses, which could explain the high number of small suppliers. Nonetheless, the data suggests that there may be opportunities for consolidation, particularly among suppliers in the £10,000 to £100,000 range.

Potential for Consolidation

Consolidating purchases with fewer, larger suppliers could potentially save the council money through bulk purchasing discounts and reduced administrative costs. For example, if the council could consolidate purchases from the 2,607 suppliers in the £10,000 to £100,000 range, it might be able to negotiate better rates with fewer suppliers. However, this would require a careful balance between efficiency and maintaining a diverse supplier base.

The data does not provide information on the nature of the goods or services provided by these suppliers, so it's difficult to assess the feasibility of consolidation. Some services may be highly specialised, making consolidation difficult or impossible. Nonetheless, the high number of small suppliers suggests that there may be opportunities for consolidation in certain areas.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to West Lancashire Borough Council about their procurement consolidation strategies
  • Attend council committee meetings to learn more about their procurement processes and ask questions
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see which suppliers are awarded contracts and for what services
  • Write to your local councillor about the potential for consolidating purchases with fewer, larger suppliers
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data and analyse the supplier distribution yourself
This article is based on West Lancashire Borough Council's published spending data covering 2016/17 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
